title: Boeing 777X Delivery Slips to 2027 With $4.9 Billion Program Charge
dek: Boeing pushed first 777X delivery to 2027 and booked a roughly $4.9 billion program charge in its third-quarter 2025 results, deepening losses on a widebody now about seven years behind its original timeline.
body:
Boeing has pushed the first delivery of its 777X widebody to 2027 and booked a program charge of roughly $4.9 billion, the company disclosed alongside its third-quarter 2025 results, deepening the financial drag on a jet that is now running about seven years behind its original schedule. The latest slip pairs a fresh delay with another substantial accounting hit, underscoring how stubbornly the flagship twin-engine program has resisted Boeing's repeated attempts to bring it across the finish line.

The 777X, the largest twin-engine airliner Boeing has developed, was once expected to enter service near the start of the decade. Instead, the program has absorbed a string of setbacks that have steadily eroded its timeline and inflated its cost, with cumulative development overruns now running into the billions. The new charge adds to that total and reflects the expense of pressing forward through a demanding certification campaign.

Certification remains the gating factor

Boeing has indicated it is targeting type certification of the 777X in the second half of 2026, with first delivery to follow in 2027. That sequencing leaves little slack: any further hiccups in flight testing or regulatory review could compress the gap between certification and handover, or push the milestones again. The program has worked through a series of technical issues during testing, the kind of late-stage hurdles that regulators expect to see fully resolved before signing off on a new widebody.

The stakes around certification have only grown in recent years, as authorities have applied heightened scrutiny to new Boeing programs. For the 777X, that means each phase of the flight-test and approval process carries weight not just for the schedule but for the credibility of Boeing's broader commercial recovery.

Every additional charge on the 777X is both a financial event and a confidence test, signaling whether Boeing can hold a line it has moved more than once.

Patient customers, mounting costs

Launch and early customers have waited years for the jet, reshaping fleet plans around a program that keeps moving to the right. Carriers that ordered the 777X for high-capacity, long-haul routes have had to lean on existing widebodies for longer than planned, while Boeing absorbs the cost of compensation and the lost momentum of a delayed entry into service.

How the 777X program reached this point

  • An original service entry once anticipated near the start of the decade.
  • A succession of technical and certification challenges during testing.
  • Cumulative development charges climbing into the billions of dollars.
  • Certification now targeted for late 2026, with delivery in 2027.

The third-quarter charge of roughly $4.9 billion lands against this backdrop, a reminder that the 777X remains a meaningful weight on Boeing's commercial results even as the company works to stabilize its wider operations and output.

What it means

The push to 2027 and the latest multibillion-dollar charge reset expectations for one of the most consequential widebody programs in the market. For Boeing, the priority now is execution: clearing certification in the targeted window and converting a long-delayed program into delivered aircraft and recognized revenue. For airlines, the revised timeline forces continued reliance on current fleets and careful capacity planning into the second half of the decade. And for the wider industry, the saga of the 777X stands as a case study in how unforgiving modern certification has become, and how costly delay can be when a flagship jet keeps slipping beyond its promised debut.
